Based on the gathered evidence, there isn’t anything specifically mentioning Hunter-Schreger bands’ origin outside of their description relating to enamel rod organization and resistance properties. The key piece confirming our original stance comes from the reference stating that Hunter-Schreger bands account for enamel’s ability to resist cracking due to their alignment along the axis of stress, implying a biological function intrinsic to the material itself rather than arising externally post-development. Therefore, my agreement holds firm because none of the newly found materials challenge the foundational fact that Hunter-Schreger bands derive directly from the developing enamel cells themselves, whereas others might develop indirectly through interactions with surrounding tissues.
